turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: submitted We'll hear argument next in Number oh oh thirteen oh seven, Larry Massanari, As- Acting Commissioner of Social Security versus the Sigmon Coal Company. Mr. Wolfson.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. Congress enacted the Coal Act to prevent the collapse of a multi employer, private health care system that had promised health care benefits to retired coal miners. Miners were in danger of losing their benefits as coal operators were selling their operations and dissolving and going out of business and shifting responsibility for their employees to other employers that were in the multi employer health care system. Congress enacted the Coal Act to stop this downward spiral. It wanted to ensure that a retired miner's benefits would be the responsibility of the operator that employed the miner, if possible, or if that operator was defunct, with one of that operator's related persons. Section ninety-seven oh one C two of the Coal Act effectuates this policy. That section sets forth the related persons who may be assigned responsibility for a miner's emp- for an operator's employees. Under a straightforward reading of that statutory language in section ninety-seven oh one C two, the commissioner may assign a miner to the direct successor in interest of a signatory operator.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well... Mr. Wolfson,

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: even the dissenting judge in the court of appeals didn't buy that.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, i- u- it is true that he did not agree with us, Mr. Chief Justice, that the plain text of the statute did not support our reading, but I have to respectfully disagree with the dissenting judge because it is our position that it does.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, d- i- it depends on the meaning you give to the word described...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It depends on...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: ninety-seven oh one C two.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It depends on the meaning that you give to the word to describe.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, you have to h- h- o- e- e- decide whether described in somehow includes a reference to as opposed to a description of.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, th- there are two possible approaches to this question, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: and that is an established common usage of the meaning describe.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: can I interrupt you for a second on just on the word described? Does the word described have any meaning other than simply refer to insofar as it refers to any of the signi- in any of the entities described in subparagraphs I two I and three I? Does it do anything other than, in fact, identify each?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It i- it refers to well, i- it it points...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: back to...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: give you a definition, for example, of a controlled group.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and and and...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: only meaning that can logically be given to the word described in the context of this statute is the one you give to it.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Th- That is that is our position, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You think there's no no difference...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: between a provision which says a member of the controlled group of corporations which includes the signatory operation, or a a more detailed description, any other person who is identified as having a partnership interest or joint venture with a signatory operator, et cetera, et cetera.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Justice Scalia...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: In the prologue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: if that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: person is and then it goes one, two three.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I want to be very precise here because...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: it's not necessary to refer to the prologue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: context of the Coal Act. But...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I understand it, there hasn't been any court that has accepted your reading so that courts, whether the person was writing in dissent or in the majority, have rejected your reading as an impermissible one.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: that?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: they they, in the end, agreed with us that er- the, Judge Murnaghan below and the DC Circuit in the RG Johnson case agreed with us in the end that the statute should be...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: given this meaning because...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: basis th- what the text says.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: statute.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Now, i- it's it's our position, though, that we disagree with those holdings, and we and it's our position that it is at minimum a permissible reading of the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: statute.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: statute that's ever been interpreted that way where it, you know, describes certain...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: in certain clauses in a later provision says any person described in such and it includes a reference to signatory?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, of course  dec-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: thought there would be something, you know, at least close to this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: closest example you can think of?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, i- I don't know about describe I do know about include certainly is not in, is not a word that is giv- ordinarily meant to give an exclusionary reading, that is, it is it's not in- include is a is a non exhaustive list.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: like is a a an exa- a canon, for example, that says something like it is permissible for a court to accept an absurdly literal meaning reading of a statute where necessary to avoid an absurd result.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, the the Court has done that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean. Right. The Court has done that. The Court has done that. I think...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Heck, we've ignored the statute entirely to...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: just last term in a case called Cornell Johnson...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Cornell Johnson versus United States,

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: the Court was presented with two meanings of the word revoke.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the statute and the other one...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It is cited in the brief, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'm more likely to think that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Congress meant what it says, and and I w- why do you assert that it is it is in- unbelievable that Congress would have meant what it said here? It is certainly the case that the that the persons who would have been most affected by the interpretation of this language that you im- you that you propose would have been the very coal mine operators who were lobbying Congress to get this thing passed.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, several things.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Now, i-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: it may not be a good policy result.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, it's not just a a question of not being a good policy result.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: disappearing and not being available...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: problem, as I understand it, in just the way you feel we should address it in in some of the bills in predecessor sessions of of the Congress in which they specifically did include successors to the signatories.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Justice Souter, there's no evidence in the background to the adoption of the Coal Act that Congress ever deliberately left behind...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean this particular statute in this session of Congress.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Congress ever deliberately left behind a provision for...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sure. You're right.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: there were many co- contentious issues involved in the framing of the Coal Act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the original coal operator had sold their operations.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: A very good reason.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And they were the major players in in this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, first of all, I mean, it's important to remember...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: best they could do.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, first, it's important to remember that the coal operators were getting very significant relief from the Coal Act, which is to say that the the members of the Bituminous Coal Op- Coal Operators Association who were at that time being forced to shoulder the costs of the retire- of the employer- the retirees of the employers who had already gone out of business, they received a a a great deal of benefit from the Coal Act because tho- because the Coal Act adopted the the approach of going back in time and reaching some of those people who had disappeared, and the problem was that they had disappeared and shifted and disappeared and nobody had been there to to to pick up the the cost.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: could have intended, and I think it...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: is entirely conceivable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You you...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You have to be very careful about...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: generalizing what is conceivable for Congress.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, if there had been some indication in the legislative background that this was a contentious issue and that there was attempt to, as the respondents have said, reclaim what was given away in the compromise, then then there might be some substance at that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Horse...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Horse trading has got to be explicit.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, it's n- i- it's not that you have to be explicit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: By the odd- oddity...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: that you're relying on an illogic between the related people and their and their successors count and not the successors of the the operator.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: That that's not right.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But you would tha- not in many cases.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I can't I can't state how many because there are sixteen thousand assignments and they are not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: organized this way.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: if this was a minimal likelihood, the other was a much more substantial risk for the coal companies.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, I think that to adopt that view, one ha- a- one would have to know for certain that related persons were necessarily not coal compa- not also coal companies.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But there's something else.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the small group, shouldn't have been included.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: first, I would not agree that we lose on the plain language of the statute.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Wolfson.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: H- How d- how do we know that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: ninety-seven eleven G one explicitly says that successors in interest...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: to signatory operators are liable for the individual employer...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: and for the ninety-two...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: plan.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: helps you rather than hurt you.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, I well, I readily acknowledge that you can make both arguments, but I think the better argument is that it helps us because it adds to the incongruity that our opponents are trying to argue for here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Combined Fund. We just don't...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: because if your opponent is right, everybody would have a motive to sell out right away.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well h-

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: right away.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, precisely, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Buscemi?

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I don't follow that because isn't it just standard corporate law that if you sell out your assets and then quit business and there are liabilities out there, that your shareholders will be stuck with that liability up to the amount that they gained from the asset sale? Isn't that just standard corporate law?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, I suspect, Your Honor, that the argument would be made that that's also not in the statute and that, therefore, you can't import that into the statute.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But isn't that why would that State law be displaced? The standard provision for a company that sells its assets and then goes out of business, for there to be shareholder liability.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it may very well not be displaced.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah, if it if it did that, it wouldn't have had to have the last the last clause of of section ninety-seven oh one C two, which says a related person shall also include a successor in interest.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, you're absolutely right, Justice Scalia, but that doesn't mean that Congress, therefore, should be penalized for including some more specific provisions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: said so in a way.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: but that meaning would be make the successor equal to the the signatory corporation, and that's not what you're urging, and that's not what Mr. Wolfson suggested the statute means.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Your Honor, as a matter of discretion, the commissioner, in assigning the beneficiaries, could elect which of several possible assigned persons it was going to make the assignment to, and I think that's all the commissioner has done here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: This Court said...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: that's also I don't understand because I'm not sure that the the when when the title one defines successor, it is talking about the person who buys assets.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, that may be, and that's what our opponents argue, but that issue is not before the Court right now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: which is...

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I think in Holy Trinity they were talking about the spirit of the...

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: statute, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Going back to your argument that there, the the other side's position is simply an an immediate inducement to sell, isn't one answer to that that if that's what the the the signatory does, it's going to be the signatory's brother sister corporations or parent corporations that are, in effect, going to m- m- probably going to be penalized, as it were, with liability? And isn't that the reason why the inducement that you refer to does not operate in such an automatic way?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, not necessarily, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Are there multiple corporations? Are there brother and sister...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: yeah, it would depend on how control is exerted, but presumably in some of these relationships, there would be a sufficient degree of control to exert that would would prevent this untoward result.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: We're not saying it would happen in each and every case, Your Honor, but I think certainly you you would see a lot of smaller companies using a transaction like this in the future to f- escape this liability.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Does the act impose potential liability on related persons of successors in interest to signatory operators?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it, again, it doesn't specifically go down that line and I'm not a- aware of any case in which you've taken the the SSA has taken a a successor to a related...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: person and then...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: one of the respondents here? A related person to a successor to a signatory?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And there, you can't point to anything in the act that would cover that.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, Your Your Honor, I I think that if if Jericol is liable,

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: then Sigmon would be liable as a related person to Jericol.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Buscemi.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: wanted the commissioner to do.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the strongest policy reason would be for imposing liabilities on successors in interest to related parties but not on successors in interest to signatory operators?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I don't know that there is any policy reason for imposing...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: them on...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Congress make that choice?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I think Congress made that choice because, as Justice Scalia has has noted, this was a statute that came togethe- came together very quickly under great pressure.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So, if it's inadvertent if it's inadvertent, I think we should try, as hard as possible, to get the interpretation they would have wanted.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I don't know,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice Breyer...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: could there be?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well the-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It makes no sense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, it it the...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: possible explanation could there be other than a legislative mistake?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: The the one possible reason why that is there is that it was never intended to have the broad meaning which the commissioner has given it,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: which is...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: why? What what what what th- you said there is no reason that you can think of.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: The I don't know why it's there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: was there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: some legislative history.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: If there's no conceivable reason, I'm I'm inclined to say, yeah, maybe you know, maybe it was a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: mistake and we should...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: we should regard it as a scrivener's error.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I think it came out that way because when you look at the parallelism of the earlier draft of the statute, there had been provisions that were parallel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: which is...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: make you think that this was intentional, but you...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Exactly. That is taken out...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: conceive of any reason why somebody would do that intentionally.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: To put in Justice Breyer's question was why might there be a reference to successor in interest of a related person...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: and not a such a reference to...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: a successor in interest of the direct signatory.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: there was a...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: never put in?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, it was...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: bill, it was never...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It was not put in. It was the from the earlier draft.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: do any ripping to the text with which they started here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Not this text, that's correct.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: go one step further than Justice Scalia's question and and and ask you this.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: section and yet leaving it in the other.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: leave it as kind of inec- unexplained inadvertence.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: It would be if it's unexplained inadvertence, it would be in leaving in language...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that speaks to successor in interest of the related person.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but you don't know which mistake it is,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, no, I think...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: failing to include the the original owners or including the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No. It it would seem clear...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the later language instead of ignoring this language.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: which language we should ignore.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That is...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: The proble- the problem is that a- and that's why the courts tend to enforce the plain language of the statute as written is because we don't know what happens in those discussions, in those negotiations that lead to the enact-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You're you're you're not saying, are you, that you know for a fact that there was not this this kind of motive, to simply save the liability of the initial coal owners? You you don't know for a fact that that was not why it was eliminated, do you?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: There is nothing in the record that speaks to it...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: one way or the other. But it's plausible...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: look to this part of the record, but there is legislative history that to me strongly suggests there was no such political deal.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well, let me explain each of those those two comments.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I agree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that they're there is evidence...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: that what you're saying is completely true, that this was not a deal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What in here indicates otherwise? Isn't that the issue? The issue is not the meaning of the of of whether one, two, and three include assigns.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Justice Breyer, the the title one dictionary act by its terms is to be applied when there is no evidence...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's right. That's exactly...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: ample evidence in this statute that Congress specifically considered both questions of successor and successors in interest because those terms are used throughout the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But what you're doing right this minute,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: you see, which is fine, which I'll let you do and I'd like you to do, but note that what you're doing is not addressing, as I think you're quite right, whether you can shoehorn successors into paragraphs one, two, and three, which is pretty tough to do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: May I...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, I don't don't blame it on him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: May may I  follow up on that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: by asking of my role...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: in saying that the dictionary act the use of the word successor in the dictionary act is not the use of the word if Justice Breyer was right that successor means successor, but I thought successor, in the meaning of the dictionary act, was not the kind of successor that we're dealing with here that is an asset sale, but was the successor corporation when two corporations merge, when one corporation acquires another.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: successor.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Judge Ginsburg, and that's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: W- Why is that?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Because what the what it says is, literally, pretend that the word successor appears in the statute that you're reading.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What in there says that I can't read this as applying to an asset sale as well as any other kind of transfer?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Again, the dictionary act is of general background relevance to the Court to provide assistance where Congress hasn't spoken to the issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I just cor-

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: L- L- Let me just...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: to not having that term appear in section ninety-seven oh one C two.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I just clarify one point in response to Ju- your answer to Justice Ginsburg? You do agree, do you not, that even though this sale was an asset sale, that we are dealing with a successor in interest of a signatory here, within the meaning of ninety-seven oh one C?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: As the commissioner has defined it, that issue as to whether...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: So, the term successor, at least in this statute, includes a purchaser that at an at an asset sale as well as a purchaser of stock.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: No, Your Honor, e- it doesn't necessarily include that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: commissioner has taken.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: conceded that you are success- your client is a suc- c- successor in interest of a s- signatory operator?

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: As as the commissioner...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: has, well,

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well, i- the issue is not before the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: is used.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: there are statutes that...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: define successor in interest to include a kind of an asset sale.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Exactly, Judge Ginsburg, and that's why it would be remanded for further determinations, although the Williams Mountain court, the the DC the District of Columbia Circuit several months ago in the case of Williams Mountain did define that term to they didn't give it a specific definition, but they said it's somewhere between a tax code successor and the Black's Law Dictionary common law successor, which does not include asset...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: purchasers.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you cannot fairly have it both ways.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: We would not rely on the dictionary act as having any relevance on remand.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: left to the dictionary act...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: successor liability in a way that you've been nu- unable to explain makes any sense whatsoever, that there's no reason to impose successor liability on the successor of a related person, while not imposing it on a successor of a signatory.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: It doesn't, the statute does not impose liability on successors.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Successors of related persons.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: expand to...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: You're suggesting there's a difference between successor in interest and successor.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Yes, I I I believe...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: there is a difference.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well, typically if you look at the Black's Law Dictionary or the common law application of successor in interest, it is statutory succession, merger, name change successors, and so forth.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the commissioner even had adopted that definition.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: assume it only refers to s- s- a limited class.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well, it it really I don't know the reason for that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: other than the the notation this morning by the Solicitor General, there we weren't aware that the commissioner had ever made...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: an assignment of liability.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: that statement in in your...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: brief was inaccurate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: that merely emphasizes the incongruity of the statute.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I don't think so, with all due respect, because what what happened or or what is a plausible scenario is that we have fifty years of retroactivity in commercial transactions that have occurred in the coal industry.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But if I understand the definition of related persons, it could cover p- people not even in the coal business at all...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and their successors in interest.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: the thing about that definition is and the reason it has some logic to expanding liability is it does encompass entities that have an economic connection with the employer.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I do I do have one question on on on the basic statutory point, the s- the shoehorning question of whether or not Shackleford is is is described in the statute.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: No, it was not.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So, then s- Roman One would not apply.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct. There are a ton of others...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Shackleford had been a member of a controlled group of corporations, then it might have been included in the controlled group and then it might have been described.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Which is the problem with the commissioner's linguistic gymnastics in trying to say that the the commissioner's definition in that case would basically mean that there's always a control group because one is always related to himself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: case.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: been a member of a controlled group, not a group of one, that argument would have been slightly more plausible, it seems to me.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: I don't think it would be any more...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: plausible through the language.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And i-, but i-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: When I talk about Shackleford, I mean the first Shackleford.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Because if it had been, I think the commissioner would have had a better argument, and it would still have the problem of being related to yourself.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: We would still have all the linguistic problems with with...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: describing...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: think all because it is the word there is include...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: It's...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: in o- one.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Well, it it's not applicable here.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I know you've discounted the significance of the legislative history, ju- the Senator's comment and so forth.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: The the jurisprudence of this Court is that the language of the statute controls when it's clear.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Even if it's perfectly clear Congress intended otherwise.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I guess the answer is that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the only way it it can be perfectly clear that Congress intended otherwise is for both houses of Congress, not a single committee of one Congress, to have enacted the provision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: perfectly clear that both houses intended otherwise.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It would make it perfectly clear to Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It it it it...

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: it makes it makes for a a rational jurisprudence when one tends to enforce the the language that's actually in the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: enactment of this act.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: makes it so anomalous to impose successor liability on successors of re- related persons who don't have anything to do with the coal industry.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: But if but if that question comes to the Court, the Court will have to deal with that question as to whether that is liability that may be enforced and imposed on such distantly related entities for historic transactions under the same criteria that the Court used, for example, in the Eastern Enterprises case.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Of course, these people were distant not only from the transactions in question but also from the negotiations that produced the statute.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And and that may well be the ex- explanation of why they got stuck.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Yes, if they weren't there to represent their interests, then a- and they likely weren't since, I don't think, anybody that is that distance, distantly related would ha- have even been aware that there was a problem in funding benefits.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But, of course, here there's no doubt about the fact that whoever is covered it was intended to be the statute was intended to have retroactive effect.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes. It is it is retroactive to everyone that is covered.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: The final point is that if this language is enforced as written, the benefits are going to be provided to the Shackleford beneficiaries on a pro rata basis by all the operators who are assigned liability, including Jericol, to the extent it o- it otherwise has valid liability for people that it did employ.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: will receive their benefits for a long...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: funding has been adequate thus far.

turns.name: JOHN R. WOODRUM
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Woodrum.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Mr. Wolfson, you have half a minute remaining.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Don't use it all in...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Very briefly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Wolfson.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: The case is submitted.

turns.name: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: The honorable court is now adjourned until Tuesday the thirteenth of November at ten o'clock.
